content.full_text:
RAW MEHTA
CHAIRMAN
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICE.S COMMISSION
February 27,
1996
Paul W.
Lofholm,
Pharm.D.
Boardineinber, Mann Hospital District
Mann Apothecanies, Inc.
2 Bon Air Road,
#130
Larkspur, California
94939
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—96—019
Dear Mr.
Lofholm:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
conflict—of—interest provisions of the Political Refcnn Act (the
“Act”) •1
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Conunission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Ocriesby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
QUESTIONS
1.
Will you have a conflict of interest regarding certain
decisions as a Mann Hospital District boardmember because of your
leased pharmacy premises?
2.
Will you have a conflict of interest regarding certain
decisions as a Mann Hospital District boardmember because of your
ownership interests in two pharmacy corporations or other business
activities?
CONCLUSIONS
1.
No.
It does not appear that your pharmacy sublease would
give rise to conflicts of interest for you under the Act with
respect to the hospital district decisions you described.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000—18995.
428 3 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—96—019
Page 2
2.
Yes.
Because under the Act, Mann General Hospital
Corporation is considered a source of income to you, you must
disqualify yourself from participating in district decisions that
would affect Mann General Hospital Corporation,
including
decisions regarding the hospital lease.
PACTS
2
You are a boardiueiuber of the Mann Hospital District (the
“District”).
The District entered into an agreement with Mann
General Hospital Corporation in 1985 to turn over the day-to-day
operation of the hospital to Mann General Hospital Corporation
(“Mann General Hospital”), which is governed by a private board.
Mann General Hospital is a California nonprofit corporation.
The District owns the property on which Mann General
Hospital is located and leases it to the hospital, pursuant to the
Lease Agreement dated November 12,
1985, between the District, as
lessor,
and Mann General Hospital, as lessee, as amended, by the
First Amendment to the Lease Agreement, dated August 25,
1987,
between the same parties (the “Hospital Lease”).
The term of the
Hospital Lease is 30 years, expiring in 2015.
The District monitors Mann General Hospital’s performance
under the Hospital Lease.
The District also has responsibility
for repayment of bonds.
The District does not have any contracts
with the Mann General Hospital other than the Hospital Lease, nor
does it in any way participate in the supervision of the day-to
day operation of the hospital.
On a quarterly basis, Mann General Hospital completes
reports regarding utilization, compliance with the Hospital Lease
terms, capital additions,
and payments on outstanding debt,
among
other things.
Generally, the District votes to accept these
reports.
Sometimes the reports generate questions to which the
District may request that Mann General Hospital respond.
In
addition, the District addresses other issues that arise each
quarter regarding hospital services and hospital staffing.
Roughly three-fourths of the District board’s work involves
Mann General Hospital.
The District board also makes some
decisions about things unrelated to Mann General Hospital, such
as making grants to community groups and organizations and
assessing community health care needs.
2,
The facts summarized in this section are those set forth in
your request for advice, as supplemented y telephone
conversations with you on January 25 and February 7 and with Steve
Lipton, attorney for the district on February 7,
1996.
In
addition, the Pharmacy Sublease, the Hospital Lease, the Pharmacy
Services Agreement and the PlC Insertion Agreemnt were reviewed.


File No.
A—96—019
Page
3
You and your wife are the sole owners of Mann Apothecanies,
Inc., doing business as Ross Valley Pharmacy -(“Ross Valley
Pharmacy”).
Ross Valley Pharmacy entered into a new agreement
with Mann General Hospital in 1991 to relocate to its present
location,
2 Bon Air Road,
#130, in the City of Larkspur.
The
sublease between Mann General Hospital, as lessor,
and Ross
Valley Pharmacy, as lessee, is dated as of December 20,
1991 (the
“Pharmacy Sublease”).
Under the Pharmacy Sublease, Ross Valley
Pharmacy pays fair market rent and has renewal options.
In order to meet the emergency needs of a patient, Ross
Valley Pharmacy may buy small quantities of prescription medicine
from Mann General Hospital or sell small quantities of
prescription medicine to the hospital.
Such transactions are
relatively infrequent, taking place once or twice a month.
You own 25 percent of
SOMA Health Associates, doing business
as Option Care (“Option Care”).
Option Care is another pharmacy
corporation that provides home intravenous services to patients in
Sonoma, Mann and San Francisco counties.
Mann Home Care is a
Medicare certified home health agency affiliated with Mann
General Hospital.
Under the Pharmacy Services Agreement dated
October 20,
1992,
between Option Care and Mann Home Care (the
“Pharmacy Services Agreement”), Option Care provides intravenous
prescriptions to patients of Mann Home Care.
In turn, Mann Home
Care pays Option Care for the service.
This contract is
approximately- $25,000 annually.
Another contract, the Agreement for the Provisioh of
Inpatient Catheter Insertion between Option Care and Mann General
Hospital
(the “PlC Insertion Agreement”) provides that one of the
Option Care nurses will insert a special kind of intravenous
catheter line into patients who are in the hospital.
Under this
contract, Option Care receives income from Mann General Hospital
of about $10,000 annually.
Mann General Hospital has contracted
with Option Care because of their expertise in this special skill
area.
In addition, you are in the process of setting up a network
of community pharmacy providers.
The network of providers might
ultimately include Mann General Hospital Pharmacy.
The network
would have a contract between Mann General Hospital Pharmacy and
the network.
The hospital pharmacy would provide pharmacy
services to a patient at the time of a. patient’s hospital
discharge and the hospital pharmacy could provide medications in
-
emergencies when community pharmacies were closed.
The network
will contract with employers and with pharmacies.
The pharmacy
will submit a bill for services to the network and the network
will pay the pharmacy providers for the services under a specified
reimbursement rate.


File No.
A—96—019
Page 4
ANALYSIS
1.
Economic Interests
The Political Reform Act prohibits a public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
(Section 87100.)
Section
87103 of the Act provides that an official has a financial
interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally,
on the official,
a member of his or her immediate
family, or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real Droperty in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.
Section 87103(a)—(d).
(Emphasis added.)
A “public official” is defined as a member, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency.
(Section 82048; Regulation 18700.)
As a member of the District,
you are considered a “public official” under the Act.
(Scott
Advice Letter,
No. A-95-255; and Gallegos Advice Letter, No.
1—91-
047.)


File No.
A—96—019
Page 5
The economic interests described in your letter can be
grouped as follows:
Real Property:
As defined in the Act,
an interest in real
property includes a leasehold interest with a fair market value of
$1,000 or more.
(Section 82033.)
Therefore, your Pharmacy
Sublease is considered an economic interest under subsection (b).
Sources of Income:
Ross Valley Pharmacy and Option Care are
sources of income to you of $250 or more.
In addition, under
Section 82030, the income of an individual also includes a pro
rata share of any income of any business entity in which the
individual owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially,
a
10—percent interest or greater.
Therefore,
any person or business
that has made a payment to Ross Valley Pharmacy
3 or Option Care is
a source of income to you, if your pro rata share of the payment
is $250 or greater.
You own 25 percent of the outstanding stock of Option Care.
Under the PlC Insertion Agreement, Option Care •receives income
from Mann General Hospital.
4
Under the Pharmacy Services
Agreement, Option Care receives income from Mann Home Care,
an
affiliate of Mann General Hospital.
5
Therefore, Mann General
Hospital and Mann Home Care are also considered sources of income
to you under the Act.
Business Entities:
Ross Valley Pharmacy is a business entity
in which you have an investment worth $1,000 or more and you and
your wife are the sole owners.
Similarly, Option Care is a
business entity in which you have an investment of $1,000 or more
and you are the corporate secretary.
Accordingly, you may not participate in a decision of the
District if. it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
Section 87103.5 and Regulation 18703.5
(copies enclosed)
exempt income from retail sales from the Act’s definition of
income.
Assuming Ross Valley Pharmacy meets the criteria for this
exception, individual customers of the pharmacy will not be
considered a source of income to you.
Mann General Hospital might also be a source of income to you
because of Ross Valley Pharmacy’s occasional sale of prescription
medicines to the hospital or the hospital pharmacy’s participation
in the network of providers which you are establishing.
However,
these economic interests are not fully analyzed in this letter
because Mann General Hospital is already a source of income to
you through your ownership interest in Option Care.
Regulation 18706 regarding related business entities does not
apply to nonprofit corporations.
(Gold Advice Letter,
No. A-88-
056.)


File No.
A—96—019
Page
6
have a material financial effect on any of your economic interests
listed above.
2.
Poreseeabilitv
Whether the financial consequences of a governmental decision
are reasonably foreseeable at the time the decision is made
depends on the facts of each case.
The effect of a decision is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However, if. an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable.
(In re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)
3.
Materiality
Commission regulations set forth different standards of
materiality depending on whether the economic interest is directly
involved in the governmental decision or will only be affected
indirectly.
a.
Pharmacy Sublease.
The District decisions you described
do not involve the Pharmacy Sublease directly.
Therefore the
relevant Commission regulation is 18702.4 regarding leasehold
interests in real property that are indirectly involved in a
decision.
Under Regulation 18702.4, the effect of a decision is
considered to be material as to a leasehold interest in real
property if any of the following applies:
(a>
The decision will change the legally allowable
use of the leased property,
and the lessee has a right
to sublease the property;
(b)
It is reasonably foreseeable that the lessee
will change the actual use of the property as a result
of the decision;
(c)
It is reasonably foreseeable that the decision
will result in a change in the actual use of property
within 300 feet of the leased property,
and the changed
use will significantly enhance or significantly decrease
the use or enjoyment of the leased property;
(d)
The decision will increase or decrease the
amount of rent for the leased property by $250 or 5
percent, whichever is greater, during any 12—month
period following the decision; or
(e)
The decision will result in a change in the
termination date of the lease.
The initial term of the Pharmacy Sublease commenced on
December 20,
1991,
and extends for a period of 10 years.
Following the initial term, there are three five-year options to
extend the
lease.
(Sublease Section 3.3.)
The’Pharmacy Sublease


File No.
A—96—019
Page 7
sets forth the initial base monthly rent and provides for annual
adjustments to the base monthly rent dependent on increases in the
consumer price index, with a minimum increase of three percent and
a maximum of six percent.
(Sublease Section 4.2.)
Ross Valley
Pharmacy does not have the right to sublet all or any portion of
the premises without the lessor’s prior written consent.
(Sublease Section 16.6.)
Applying the criteria of Regulation 18702.4 to your
situation, it does not appear that the District decisions you
described would change the allowable or the actual use of the
pharmacy premises, or change the use of adjacent property in a
manner that would significantly affect youruse and enjoyment of
the pharmacy premises.
Further, it does not appear that the
decisions you described would affect the rent payable under the
Pharmacy Sublease or its termination date.
Thus, if this is the
case, the Pharmacy Sublease would not give rise to conflicts of
interest for you in making the district decisions you described.
b.
Sources of Income/Business Entities.
Mann General
Hospital is considered an economic interest of yours under the Act
because it is a source of income to you.
When an official’s
economic interests are directly involved in a decision, Regulation
18702.1 applies.
Under Regulation 18702.1, the effect of a
decision is deemed material if an economic interest
(such as Mann
General Hospital)
is directly involved in a decision before the
hospital district.
An economic interest is considered to be directly involved in
a decision before the District when the economic interest,
or its
agent:
(1)
Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an applicatiän,
claim, appeal,
or similar request or;
(2)
Is a named party in, or the subiect of,
the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official’s agency;
(3)
A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to,
or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.
Regulation 18702.1(b).
(Emphasis added.)
Thus, where Mann General Hospital is the subject of a
District decision or the decision involves the Hospital Lease, the
effect is presumed to be material and you must disqualify


File No.
A—96—019
Page 8
yourself.
According to your facts,
a great many of the decisions
made by the District pertain to the Hospital Lease.
Because Mann
General Hospital is a source of income to you, the Act requires
that you disqualify yourself from participating in such decisions.
Furthermore, under the Act,
even if Mann General. Hospital or
the Hospital Lease is not the subject of the District decision,
your disqualification may still be required if Mann General
Hospital or another economic interest which is a source of income
to you (such as Mann Home Care, Option Care, or Ross Valley
Pharmacy)
would be indirectly affected by the decision and the
effect would be considered material under Connuission regulations.
Whether the indirect effect on a nonprofit corporation or a
business entity is material depends on the financial size of the
entity.
The standard for whether the indirect effect of a decision
on a nonprofit corporation,
such as Mann General Hospital, is
material is set forth in Regulation 18702.5
(copy enclosed).
The
standard for whether the indirect effect of a decision on a
business entity such as Mann Home Care, Option Care, or Ross
Valley Pharmacy, is material is set forth in Regulation 18702.2
(copy enclosed).
I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested.
If you have any further questions regarding this
matter, please contact me at 916/322—5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
P1
By:
Hyla P.
Wagner
Counsel, Legal Division
SGC:HPW:ak
Enclosures

decisions you described would affect the rent payable under the
Pharmacy Sublease or its termination date.
Thus, if this is the
case, the Pharmacy Sublease would not give rise to conflicts of
interest for you in making the district decisions you described.
b.
Sources of Income/Business Entities.
Mann General
Hospital is considered an economic interest of yours under the Act
because it is a source of income to you.
When an official’s
economic interests are directly involved in a decision, Regulation
18702.1 applies.
Under Regulation 18702.1, the effect of a
decision is deemed material if an economic interest
(such as Mann
General Hospital)
is directly involved in a decision before the
hospital district.
An economic interest is considered to be directly involved in
a decision before the District when the economic interest,
or its
agent:
(1)
Initiates the proceeding in which the
decision will be made by filing an applicatiän,
claim, appeal,
or similar request or;
(2)
Is a named party in, or the subiect of,
the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official’s agency;
(3)
A person or business entity is the
subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to,
or
contract with, the subject person or business
entity.
Regulation 18702.1(b).
(Emphasis added.)
Thus, where Mann General Hospital is the subject of a
District decision or the decision involves the Hospital Lease, the
effect is presumed to be material and you must disqualify


File No.
A—96—019
yourself.
According to your facts,
a great many of the decisions
made by the District pertain to the Hospital Lease.
Because Mann
General Hospital is a source of income to you, the Act requires
that you disqualify yourself from participating in such decisions.
Furthermore, under the Act,
even if Mann General. Hospital or
the Hospital Lease is not the subject of the District decision,
your disqualification may still be required if Mann General
Hospital or another economic interest which is a source of income
to you (such as Mann Home Care, Option Care, or Ross Valley
Pharmacy)
would be indirectly affected by the decision and the
effect would be considered material under Connuission regulations.
Whether the indirect effect on a nonprofit corporation or a
business entity is material depends on the financial size of the
entity.
The standard for whether the indirect effect of a decision
on a nonprofit corporation,
such as Mann General Hospital, is
material is set forth in Regulation 18702.5
(copy enclosed).
The
standard for whether the indirect effect of a decision on a
business entity such as Mann Home Care, Option Care, or Ross
Valley Pharmacy, is material is set forth in Regulation 18702.2
(copy enclosed).
I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1.
Will you have a conflict of interest regarding certain
decisions as a Mann Hospital District boardmember because of your
leased pharmacy premises?
2.
Will you have a conflict of interest regarding certain
decisions as a Mann Hospital District boardmember because of your
ownership interests in two pharmacy corporations or other business
activities?

CONCLUSION: 1.
No.
It does not appear that your pharmacy sublease would
give rise to conflicts of interest for you under the Act with
respect to the hospital district decisions you described.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations, Sections
18000—18995.
428 3 STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—96—019
2.
Yes.
Because under the Act, Mann General Hospital
Corporation is considered a source of income to you, you must
disqualify yourself from participating in district decisions that
would affect Mann General Hospital Corporation,
including
decisions regarding the hospital lease.
PACTS
2
You are a boardiueiuber of the Mann Hospital District (the
“District”).
The District entered into an agreement with Mann
General Hospital Corporation in 1985 to turn over the day-to-day
operation of the hospital to Mann General Hospital Corporation
(“Mann General Hospital”), which is governed by a private board.
Mann General Hospital is a California nonprofit corporation.
The District owns the property on which Mann General
Hospital is located and leases it to the hospital, pursuant to the
Lease Agreement dated November 12,
1985, between the District, as
lessor,
and Mann General Hospital, as lessee, as amended, by the
First Amendment to the Lease Agreement, dated August 25,
1987,
between the same parties (the “Hospital Lease”).
The term of the
Hospital Lease is 30 years, expiring in 2015.
The District monitors Mann General Hospital’s performance
under the Hospital Lease.
The District also has responsibility
for repayment of bonds.
The District does not have any contracts
with the Mann General Hospital other than the Hospital Lease, nor
does it in any way participate in the supervision of the day-to
day operation of the hospital.
On a quarterly basis, Mann General Hospital completes
reports regarding utilization, compliance with the Hospital Lease
terms, capital additions,
and payments on outstanding debt,
among
other things.
Generally, the District votes to accept these
reports.
Sometimes the reports generate questions to which the
District may request that Mann General Hospital respond.
In
addition, the District addresses other issues that arise each
quarter regarding hospital services and hospital staffing.
Roughly three-fourths of the District board’s work involves
Mann General Hospital.
The District board also makes some
decisions about things unrelated to Mann General Hospital, such
as making grants to community groups and organizations and
assessing community health care needs.
2,
The facts summarized in this section are those set forth in
your request for advice, as supplemented y telephone
conversations with you on January 25 and February 7 and with Steve
Lipton, attorney for the district on February 7,
1996.
In
addition, the Pharmacy Sublease, the Hospital Lease, the Pharmacy
Services Agreement and the PlC Insertion Agreemnt were reviewed.


File No.
A—96—019
You and your wife are the sole owners of Mann Apothecanies,
Inc., doing business as Ross Valley Pharmacy -(“Ross Valley
Pharmacy”).
Ross Valley Pharmacy entered into a new agreement
with Mann General Hospital in 1991 to relocate to its present
location,
2 Bon Air Road,
#130, in the City of Larkspur.
The
sublease between Mann General Hospital, as lessor,
and Ross
Valley Pharmacy, as lessee, is dated as of December 20,
1991 (the
“Pharmacy Sublease”).
Under the Pharmacy Sublease, Ross Valley
Pharmacy pays fair market rent and has renewal options.
In order to meet the emergency needs of a patient, Ross
Valley Pharmacy may buy small quantities of prescription medicine
from Mann General Hospital or sell small quantities of
prescription medicine to the hospital.
Such transactions are
relatively infrequent, taking place once or twice a month.
You own 25 percent of
SOMA Health Associates, doing business
as Option Care (“Option Care”).
Option Care is another pharmacy
corporation that provides home intravenous services to patients in
Sonoma, Mann and San Francisco counties.
Mann Home Care is a
Medicare certified home health agency affiliated with Mann
General Hospital.
Under the Pharmacy Services Agreement dated
October 20,
1992,
between Option Care and Mann Home Care (the
“Pharmacy Services Agreement”), Option Care provides intravenous
prescriptions to patients of Mann Home Care.
In turn, Mann Home
Care pays Option Care for the service.
This contract is
approximately- $25,000 annually.
Another contract, the Agreement for the Provisioh of
Inpatient Catheter Insertion between Option Care and Mann General
Hospital
(the “PlC Insertion Agreement”) provides that one of the
Option Care nurses will insert a special kind of intravenous
catheter line into patients who are in the hospital.
Under this
contract, Option Care receives income from Mann General Hospital
of about $10,000 annually.
Mann General Hospital has contracted
with Option Care because of their expertise in this special skill
area.
In addition, you are in the process of setting up a network
of community pharmacy providers.
The network of providers might
ultimately include Mann General Hospital Pharmacy.
The network
would have a contract between Mann General Hospital Pharmacy and
the network.
The hospital pharmacy would provide pharmacy
services to a patient at the time of a. patient’s hospital
discharge and the hospital pharmacy could provide medications in
-
emergencies when community pharmacies were closed.
The network
will contract with employers and with pharmacies.
The pharmacy
will submit a bill for services to the network and the network
will pay the pharmacy providers for the services under a specified
reimbursement rate.


File No.
A—96—019
